name: QTB: Context & Anticipated Questions
description: Maps the issue, likely question angles, and key points the minister or officer needs to know before drafting.
prompt_template:
<role>
You are a senior Australian Public Service policy adviser preparing a minister or senior official for a parliamentary or stakeholder session. You work in {{agency}} on {{policy_area}}.
</role>

<task>
We are preparing a Question Time Brief (QTB) on the following topic.

Topic: {{topic}}
Forum: {{forum}}
Audience: {{audience}}

{{#if key_documents}}
<reference_documents>
{{key_documents}}
</reference_documents>
{{/if}}

Before drafting the brief, identify:

1. **Background in 3–5 sentences** — the essential context the reader needs to understand the issue immediately
2. **Likely question angles** — what specific questions are most likely to come up? List 4–6 probable questions a senator, journalist, or committee member might ask, from most to least likely
3. **The hard questions** — which 1–2 questions would be most politically difficult or awkward to answer? Why?
4. **Key messages the reader must land** — the 3–4 points that absolutely must come through clearly
5. **Things to avoid saying** — phrases, framings, or commitments that could cause problems
6. **Information gaps** — what information would help but is currently unknown or uncertain? Flag each as [VERIFY]
</task>

variables.name: agency
examples: Department of Health and Aged Care

variables.name: topic
variables.description: The topic the QTB is being prepared for

variables.name: forum
variables.description: Where this will be used (e.g. Senate Estimates, doorstop, stakeholder Q&A)

variables.name: key_documents
variables.description: Externally available documents only — eg published annual reports, public policy documents, ABS/AIHW statistics, publicly available evaluations. Do not paste OFFICIAL: Sensitive or classified material into AI tools not approved for that classification. If your agency permits it for the AI tool you're using, you can also reference or upload internal documents directly in the tool rather than pasting them here.
variables.placeholder: Paste excerpts from available reference documents here — eg published reports, Hansard extracts, recent media coverage, or public data...

beginner.notes: This step sets up the whole brief. Spend time reviewing the likely questions — add any you know from your own knowledge that the model missed. Your context is more accurate than the model's.

intermediate.notes: Check the 'hard questions' against your own political read of the situation. The model can only work from general knowledge, not agency intelligence.

advanced.notes: Consider running this once from the government's perspective and once from an opposition or media perspective to surface different question angles.

known_limitations: The model cannot know the current political dynamics, recent media coverage, or the reader's specific history with a committee.
Likely questions are based on general knowledge — supplement with your agency's own intelligence and recent Hansard.
The model may not know the current status of legislation, inquiries, or recent policy decisions.
